reasoning: The Lean formalization uses `b ≥ 1` rather than the conventional `b ≥ 2` for bases, but this is logically equivalent in context. For b = 1, `Nat.digits 1 n = List.replicate n 1` has length 3 iff n = 3, and set_lean(3) = {1}, cardinality 1 (since 3 has only 1 or 2 digits in any base ≥ 2). So any n witnessing the Lean statement must have n ≠ 3, and for all n ≠ 3, b = 1 is excluded by the length condition, making set_lean(n) = set_informal(n). Thus informal ⟹ Lean trivially (subset), and Lean ⟹ informal because any Lean-witness n satisfies n ≠ 3 and thus equals the informal set. Mathlib's `Nat.digits` convention (little-endian with nonzero most significant digit) correctly captures "3-digit" (length = 3 with no leading zero), and `List.Palindrome` matches the "reads the same backwards and forwards" condition. n = 0 gives the empty digit list in any base, so cannot witness, naturally restricting to positive integers.
